mappings.value: Omega-tilde sub i equals the long-time limit of one over t times the integral from zero to t of theta-dot sub i. Here theta-dot sub i is the instantaneous angular velocity of oscillator i, and omega-tilde sub i is its long-time average frequency.

Equation 6 defines the right frequency notion for synchronization questions. Even when phases keep moving around the circle, oscillators can still be frequency locked if their long-time average frequencies agree.
So this equation shifts the focus from instantaneous motion to asymptotic behavior. It tells us how to decide whether oscillators have settled into a common rotational rate.
Use Eq. 6 when you want a frequency-based notion of locking rather than a snapshot of phases. It is the natural definition behind later discussions of synchronized and drifting populations.
